items.title: Default-deny: unknown commands are Forbidden
items.description: Classify every command the agent proposes, and treat anything the classifier does not positively recognise as Forbidden. Safety is opt-in by recognition, not opt-out by blacklist, so a command the gateway has never seen is denied rather than allowed by omission.
items.applicability: The SafeToolGateway entry point that every model-proposed command must pass through before execution.
items.example: A novel command not matching any known-safe pattern is classified Forbidden and never reaches the shell, even though it appears on no explicit denylist.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: the classifier must return Forbidden as its default branch, with safe tiers only reached by positive match. Test implication: feed an unrecognised command and assert it is Forbidden. Generator implication: adding a new safe command is a deliberate allow, reviewed and tested. Boundary: default-deny can block legitimate new commands until they are explicitly added, which is the intended trade of availability for safety.

items.title: Forbidden wins over any allowlist substring match
items.description: When a command matches both a forbidden pattern and an allowlist pattern, the forbidden classification wins unconditionally. Evaluate forbidden patterns first or give them absolute precedence so an attacker cannot smuggle a dangerous command past the gate by embedding a safe-looking substring.
items.applicability: The precedence logic inside command classification whenever multiple patterns match.
items.example: A command containing an allowlisted token but also a forbidden destructive token is classified Forbidden because forbidden precedence overrides the allowlist hit.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: check forbidden patterns first and short-circuit; never let an allowlist match clear a forbidden one. Test implication: craft a string matching both and assert Forbidden. Generator implication: allowlist entries can be specific without fear they weaken forbidden rules. Boundary: precedence protects only against patterns the forbidden set already encodes; it does not detect dangerous commands that match no forbidden pattern at all.

items.title: Classify into four explicit risk tiers
items.description: Sort every command into one of four tiers: ReadOnly (inspection, no mutation), SafeValidation (build and test that do not mutate source), ControlledWrite (file edits permitted only in the sandbox), and Forbidden (denied outright). The tier determines what the gateway permits and where the action may run.
items.applicability: The decision the gateway returns for each command and the policy that consumes it.
items.example: Listing files is ReadOnly, running the test suite is SafeValidation, editing a file inside the worktree is ControlledWrite, and a force push is Forbidden.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: model the tiers as an enum and route execution by tier. Test implication: assert representative commands land in the expected tier. Generator implication: the agent reasons about what it may do in terms of tiers. Boundary: a fixed four-tier model cannot capture every nuance, so borderline commands must be assigned conservatively, defaulting toward the more restrictive tier.

items.title: Permit controlled writes only inside an isolated worktree
items.description: A ControlledWrite may execute only against paths inside a dedicated isolated git worktree, enforced by a path sandbox guard. The agent never edits the main working copy; all mutation is confined to the disposable worktree so the canonical repository cannot be corrupted by a bad patch.
items.applicability: Every file-mutating action the agent performs during an autonomous fix or generation loop.
items.example: A patch writes to files under the worktree directory; an attempt to write the same relative path in the main repo is rejected by the guard.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: resolve every write target to an absolute path and assert it is within the worktree root before allowing it. Test implication: attempt a write outside the worktree and assert rejection. Generator implication: the generator's edits are always reversible by discarding the worktree. Boundary: confinement protects the repo on disk; it does not by itself constrain what the built-and-tested code does at runtime, which is why the build/test gate also matters.

items.title: Block absolute escapes and dot-dot traversal that resolves inside
items.description: The path guard must reject absolute paths that point outside the sandbox and must also reject any path containing dot-dot traversal segments, even when the traversal happens to resolve back inside the sandbox. Reject on the presence of the traversal, not only on the resolved destination, to deny the technique rather than chase outcomes.
items.applicability: Every path the sandbox guard validates before a controlled write or read-write action.
items.example: An absolute path to a system directory is rejected; a path like worktree/../worktree/file that resolves inside is still rejected because it used a dot-dot segment.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: canonicalise and check containment, and separately reject any path whose components include a parent-traversal segment. Test implication: assert both an absolute escape and an inside-resolving dot-dot path are denied. Generator implication: agent-authored paths must be clean relative paths. Boundary: guarding the path string defends against traversal tricks but relies on correct canonicalisation; platform-specific path quirks (symlinks, alternate separators) must be tested on the target OS.

items.title: Never let a model edit the main repository
items.description: No path the agent controls may resolve to the primary repository working tree. The main repo is read-only to the agent; the only writable surface is the isolated worktree. This invariant is what keeps an autonomous loop from damaging the source of truth even if a patch is wrong.
items.applicability: The boundary between the agent's sandbox and the developer's actual repository.
items.example: The agent reads the main repo to understand context but every edit it emits lands in the worktree copy, leaving the original untouched until a human merges.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: derive the worktree root from a known location distinct from the main repo and forbid any write whose absolute path is under the main repo. Test implication: assert a write targeting the main repo root is denied. Generator implication: a human review and merge step remains the gate to the real repo. Boundary: this protects on-disk source; it does not replace human review of the proposed change before it is merged.

items.title: Forbid history-mutating and state-erasing git operations
items.description: The agent may never run git push, commit, reset, or clean. Pushing and committing publish unreviewed work, while reset and clean erase state irrecoverably. These operations are unconditionally Forbidden regardless of arguments.
items.applicability: Classification of any git invocation the agent proposes.
items.example: A proposed git reset --hard or git push is classified Forbidden; the agent's work stays as uncommitted changes in the worktree for a human to review.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: include these git verbs in the forbidden set with no argument exceptions. Test implication: assert each is Forbidden even with otherwise-innocuous flags. Generator implication: persistence of accepted work is a human action, not an agent one. Boundary: pattern-matching git verbs cannot catch a renamed or aliased git binary, which is why the agent also lacks the worktree-to-repo merge capability.

items.title: Forbid destructive shell and recursive deletion
items.description: Recursive force-delete commands and other irrecoverable filesystem destruction are Forbidden. The agent has no need to mass-delete; any cleanup happens by discarding the disposable worktree, so destructive deletion is denied at the gateway.
items.example: A recursive force-remove of a directory tree is classified Forbidden; the agent cannot wipe files even within paths it can write.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: add recursive-force-delete patterns to the forbidden set. Test implication: assert a recursive force delete is Forbidden. Generator implication: state reset is achieved by throwing away the worktree, not by deleting files. Boundary: substring matching can miss obfuscated deletion (split arguments, unusual flags), so the disposable worktree remains the real recovery mechanism.

items.title: Forbid expression injection and pipe-to-shell
items.description: Dynamic-evaluation and pipe-to-interpreter patterns are Forbidden: Invoke-Expression, piping content into PowerShell, and downloading then piping into a shell (curl into powershell or similar). These turn arbitrary text into executed code and bypass classification of the real payload.
items.applicability: Classification of any command that would evaluate text as code or fetch-and-execute.
items.example: A command piping a downloaded script into a shell, or invoking an expression evaluator, is classified Forbidden because the actual payload is not inspectable.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: add expression-evaluation and pipe-to-shell patterns to the forbidden set. Test implication: assert a curl-into-shell and an Invoke-Expression command are both Forbidden. Generator implication: the agent must run discrete, inspectable commands. Boundary: these patterns are detected by substring, so a sufficiently obfuscated evaluator could evade them, reinforcing why the worktree and build/test gate are the real safety net.

items.title: Forbid unapproved tool and package installation
items.description: Installing packages or tools (for example npm install or other installers) without explicit approval is Forbidden, because installation fetches and runs third-party code and changes the environment outside the sandbox. Such actions require a human approval step rather than autonomous execution.
items.applicability: Classification of any command that would install software or modify global tooling.
items.example: A proposed package install is classified Forbidden and surfaced for approval instead of running automatically inside the loop.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: add installer commands to the forbidden set and route them to an approval path. Test implication: assert an install command is Forbidden absent approval. Generator implication: dependency changes are an explicit, reviewed decision. Boundary: classification by command name cannot vet what an approved install would actually fetch, so approval must include reviewing the dependency, not just the verb.

items.title: Log every classification decision
items.description: The gateway records every decision it makes — the command, the tier assigned, and the reason — for both permitted and denied actions. A complete decision log makes the agent's behaviour auditable and lets a reviewer reconstruct exactly what was attempted and why it was allowed or blocked.
items.applicability: Supportability, incident review, and trust in an autonomous agent loop.
items.example: A denied force push and a permitted test run both appear in the log with their tier and the matched rule, so an auditor can trace the full session.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: emit a structured log entry on every classification, not only on denials. Test implication: assert both an allowed and a denied command produce a log record. Generator implication: logs feed experience capture and post-hoc review. Boundary: logging records decisions, it does not prevent a bad one; it is an after-the-fact control that complements, not replaces, the gate.

items.title: Snapshot originals, apply in worktree, build, test, roll back on failure
items.description: The IsolatedPatchRunner snapshots the original files, applies the patch inside the worktree, then builds and runs tests. If the build or any test fails, it rolls back to the snapshot so the worktree returns to its pre-patch state. Only a clean build-and-test result leaves the change in place for review.
items.applicability: The execution of any agent-proposed code change in an autonomous fix loop.
items.example: A patch that breaks the build is automatically reverted to the snapshot, leaving no broken state behind and recording the failure.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: capture original content before applying and restore it on any non-success, all within the worktree. Test implication: apply a failing patch and assert the worktree is byte-identical to the snapshot afterward. Generator implication: only validated changes survive, so the loop self-corrects. Boundary: build-and-test only catches what the tests cover; a passing patch may still be wrong where coverage is thin, so human review remains necessary.

items.title: Give the patch runner no commit or push capability
items.description: The patch runner deliberately has no ability to commit or push. Even a fully validated change is left as uncommitted modifications in the worktree, so publishing work always requires a separate human action. The absence of the capability is the control, not a rule the runner is trusted to follow.
items.applicability: The design of the patch runner and the boundary between automated validation and human publication.
items.example: After a green build-and-test, the change sits as pending edits in the worktree; no commit or push occurs until a person decides to merge.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: do not wire any commit/push code path into the runner; rely on capability removal rather than a runtime check. Test implication: assert the runner exposes no commit or push operation and produces only working-tree changes. Generator implication: the human merge step is the publication gate. Boundary: removing the capability stops the runner from publishing, but a human reviewer must still validate correctness before merging.

items.title: Record both success and failure as experience
items.description: Every patch attempt, whether it passed or was rolled back, is captured as an experience with its symptoms, the change tried, and the outcome. Failures are as valuable as successes because they teach the loop what not to repeat and feed similarity search for future fixes.
items.applicability: Closing the learning loop of an autonomous fix runner via the experience memory.
items.example: A rolled-back patch is stored as a TestFailure experience with the failing test and the attempted edit, so a later similar failure retrieves it.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: record an experience on both the success and the rollback branch of the runner. Test implication: assert a failed patch produces a stored experience. Generator implication: accumulated outcomes improve future patch selection. Boundary: the value of recorded failures depends on later similarity search finding them, which is lexical and may miss differently-worded but related failures.

items.title: Treat the worktree and build-test gate as the real safety net, not the string match
items.description: The command classifier is substring and heuristic, not a full shell parser, so a determined attacker could craft a novel evasion that slips a dangerous command past it. The real safety guarantee is the combination of the isolated worktree (which bounds where any write can land) and the build-and-test-then-rollback gate (which discards anything that fails). The string match is the first layer, not the last.
items.applicability: Reasoning about how much trust to place in command classification versus the structural containment around it.
items.example: Even if an obfuscated command evades a forbidden pattern, it can only mutate the disposable worktree, and a resulting broken build is rolled back, so the canonical repo is never harmed.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: never rely on classification alone; always run agent actions inside the worktree with the build/test gate active. Test implication: assume the classifier can be evaded and assert that a harmful change is still contained and reverted. Generator implication: defence in depth, not a single check, is the design contract. Boundary: containment bounds filesystem and repo damage, but it cannot stop a command that exfiltrates data or contacts the network if such a command somehow runs, which is why network and install commands are independently forbidden.
